chapters.title: A Child at Last
chapters.text:
Long ago a king and queen ruled a green and gentle land, and they had everything their hearts could wish for — save one. Year after year they longed for a child, and none was born to them, and that one sorrow followed them like a shadow.
Then at last, one spring, a daughter was born — so bright and fair that the king wept for joy and named her Aurora, for she had come like the dawn. He proclaimed a great feast, and invited all the realm to rejoice.
The King: “A daughter! After all these years — a daughter! Let every bell in the kingdom ring!”
The Queen: “Our little Aurora. May she be as happy as she has made us this day.”
To be the child's godmothers the king invited the good fairies of the land, for their blessings were the surest good fortune a christening could have. Three came in gowns of rose and gold and green: Rosalind, Marigold, and gentle Wren.

chapters.title: The Gifts and the Curse
chapters.text:
One by one the good fairies bent over the cradle to give their gifts. Rosalind touched the child's brow, and Marigold her lips, and each gift was lovelier than the last.
Rosalind the Good Fairy: “My gift is beauty — that all who look on her shall love her.”
Marigold the Good Fairy: “And mine is a voice of song, and a heart both kind and merry.”
But before gentle Wren could give her gift, the great doors flew open with a wind that put out half the candles. In swept Carabosse, the dark fairy, whom the king had forgotten to invite — and she had not forgotten it.
Carabosse, the Dark Fairy: “No invitation for me? Then hear my gift, little princess. On your sixteenth birthday you shall prick your finger upon a spindle — and die.”
The Queen: “No! Have mercy — she is only a babe!”
Carabosse, the Dark Fairy: “Mercy? You had none to spare for me. Sixteen years — and then the dark.”
Carabosse vanished in a gust of cold. The court stood frozen in horror — until gentle Wren stepped forward. She could not undo so strong a curse, but she had not yet given her own gift, and into it she poured all her kindness.
Wren the Good Fairy: “I cannot lift her curse, but I can soften it. Aurora shall not die. She shall fall into a deep sleep of a hundred years — and wake, at the last, to true and faithful love.”

chapters.title: The Banished Spindles
chapters.text:
That very hour the king commanded that every spindle and spinning wheel in the realm be gathered and burned, that no such thing should ever come near his daughter. And so it was done, to the last.
The years passed sweetly. Aurora grew tall and kind, with her mother's gentle heart and all the fairies' gifts upon her; and the whole kingdom loved her, and no shadow of the curse ever crossed her happy days.
Princess Aurora: “What a morning! The whole garden is in bloom. I feel as though something wonderful is waiting for me today.”
Princess Aurora: “Sixteen today — Mother says I am grown at last. And yet, of all this great castle, there is one grey tower I have never once been let inside.”
Princess Aurora: “I'll not waste such a morning indoors. Let me follow that little winding stair — just once, before the feast begins.”

chapters.title: The Spindle
chapters.text:
On the morning of her sixteenth birthday, while the king and queen were from home, Aurora wandered where she had never gone before — up a narrow winding stair to a little door she had never seen, at the very top of an old grey tower.
The Old Spinner: “Come in, come in, my dear. Don't mind an old woman at her work.”
Princess Aurora: “Good morning! But — what a strange little wheel. What is that you are doing?”
The Old Spinner: “Why, I am spinning, child. Would you like to try? Just take the spindle — so.”
Princess Aurora: “May I truly? I have never once seen thread spun in all my life. Show me how to hold it — I'll be ever so careful.”
Aurora reached out her hand — and the moment her finger touched the point of the spindle, the old curse woke. A single drop of blood, a soft cry, and she sank down as if the whole weight of a hundred years had come upon her all at once.
The good fairies felt it across the land and came flying. They could not wake her — but gentle Wren would not let her sleep alone. She touched her wand to every wall, and the whole castle slept with her.

chapters.title: The Hundred Years
chapters.text:
Around the sleeping castle a hedge of thorns began to grow, taller and thicker with every year, until towers and walls were lost in it and only the highest banner showed above the green. And within, nothing stirred; the fire stood still on the hearth, and the very dust hung waiting in the air.
A hundred years is a long time, and the story of the sleeping princess passed from grandmothers to grandchildren all across the land. Now and then a bold prince came to cut through the thorns and win her — but the hedge closed about them like cruel hands, and none came out again.

chapters.title: The Prince and the Hedge
chapters.text:
At last the hundred years were nearly spent. A young prince named Florimund, riding through the deep forest, met an old man who told him the tale of the castle behind the thorns, and of the fairest princess in the world who slept within.
Prince Florimund: “A hundred years, you say? Then the time is all but come. Let others have turned back — I will go to her.”
But as Prince Florimund came to the terrible hedge, the last day of the hundred years had dawned. The thorns before him burst into great fragrant roses and drew aside of their own accord, and closed softly behind him, and he walked through unharmed.
Through the sleeping castle he went, past guards asleep on their spears, past a cook with his hand still raised to strike, past hounds and horses and doves all dreaming — up the winding stair, to a little door, at the very top of the tower.

chapters.title: The Awakening
chapters.text:
There, in the quiet of a hundred years, lay Aurora — as fair as the day she had fallen, the faint colour of a rose still in her cheek. Florimund knelt beside her, and the last hour of the enchantment ran out like sand.
Prince Florimund: “A hundred years the world has waited for you. Wake now, sweet princess — the morning has come at last.”
Princess Aurora: “Oh... have I slept so long? Is it you — the one the good fairy promised? I feel as though I have known you in every dream.”
Princess Aurora: “A hundred years — and still you came. Then it was all true, every word the good fairy whispered over my cradle. Rise with me — I hear the whole castle waking.”
And as her eyes opened, the whole castle woke with her — the fire leaped up, the guards blinked upon their spears, the doves took wing. The good fairies laughed for joy, and there was such gladness in that place as no hundred years of sleep could hold. Now — spell the word the enchantment kept for this very hour.

chapters.title: And Joy Woke With Them
chapters.text: The whole castle woke as if from a single night's rest — the king and queen, the courtiers, the horses in the yard, the very fire on the hearth. The thorns fell into a drift of roses. And Aurora and Florimund were wed that same day, and lived long and gladly, and told the tale to their own children, who did not believe a word of it — until they were old enough to.
